content:
Just shipped real-time nested threads globally. Your conversations can now go 6 levels deep.

This has been the #1 most requested feature for three years. Starting to roll out now — 100% of users over the next 48 hours. 🧵

Echo now has 480 million monthly active users. We started in a garage 8 years ago with a feature no one wanted.

We're just getting started.

We've updated how the algorithm works for the Following tab.

Chronological is back. No ranking. No ads. Pure signal.

For the For You tab — the ranking model now weighs reply quality, not just speed. Thoughtful replies surface more.
Context window for Echo posts is now 2,800 characters. Because some ideas don't fit in 280.

Hot take: most foundation models today are sophisticated compression algorithms with a marketing department.

The interesting research is still at the reasoning layer. We're nowhere near AGI. We're near very impressive autocomplete. #AIFirst #ML

Our new paper is up: 'Emergent Reasoning in Sparse Transformer Architectures at Sub-70B Scale'.

TLDR: models start doing something that looks a lot like planning around 48B parameters, not 70B+ as previously thought. The capacity is there earlier than we assumed.

Link in bio. Still preprint but excited to share.

Just gave my first keynote at #ParaNetConf2026. Two years ago I couldn't present to a room of 10 people without losing my train of thought.

Progress is real. It's slow. It's worth it.
The "AI will replace programmers" discourse is being driven by people who have never read a bug report.

5 years ago I had $0 MRR and $0 in savings. Last month Meridian crossed $2.1M ARR.

The in-between is 1,825 days of uncomfortable work. I'm not going to romanticize it. It was hard in ways I didn't expect.

But it was worth it. And it can be worth it for you too. #BuildInPublic

Things VCs won't tell you:

• They're pattern-matching, not reasoning about your specific situation
• 'We'd love to stay in touch' usually means no
• A soft yes is a hard no with extra steps
• The check size matters as much as the name on the term sheet

This took me two fundraises to learn.

Closed our Series B today. $28M led by @ryanpark_vc at Sequoia.

The whole Meridian team has been unbelievably gritty through 18 months of tough market conditions. This validates every late night and early morning.

Now we ship.

The best product advice I ever got: ship something embarrassingly small. Then make it less embarrassing every week.

Most founders skip the embarrassingly small part. That skip is the death of most products.

The reason most design systems fail isn't technical.

It's because someone built a Figma library, called it a design system, and went back to their regular work. A design system is a *living agreement* between design and engineering. It needs an owner, a roadmap, and a feedback loop.

After 7 years at Apple I have exactly one thing burned into my soul:

Boring typography that works > exciting typography that distracts.

Every. Single. Time.

Redesigned our onboarding flow completely. Result: completion rate went from 71% to 94%.

The key insight: people don't read, they scan. We cut the word count by 70%, added visual anchors, and moved the first 'aha moment' from step 7 to step 2.

Data attached 👇

Hot take: 'mobile first' was never about designing for mobile first. It was about designing for constraints first.

Constraints produce clarity. Clarity produces good design.
The TypeScript type system is Turing complete and if you've ever discovered this at 2am debugging a library's internal types you know exactly what I mean

After 3 months of work: graphql-codegen-ts v3 is out.

Biggest changes:
— 60% faster code generation via incremental compilation
— Type-safe query variables (finally)
— First-class React Server Components support
— Zero config for Vite/Next.js

Full changelog 👇

Code review is fundamentally a teaching tool, not a quality gate. The best PRs I've ever gotten approved were the ones where I learned something from the review process, not just from writing the code.
Big story dropping tomorrow: exclusive look at the internal crisis at [REDACTED] that's been going on for 3 months. 7 sources, 400 pages of documents. Setting alarm for 6am ET.

For context on the AI chip supply chain story I've been covering: there are currently 6 major bottlenecks between a neural network design and a deployed model. 3 of them have single points of failure. This is why 'we'll just train bigger' isn't a strategy, it's a hope.

New piece: 'The Quiet Death of the Open Web' — on how every major platform shift in the past decade has made it harder for independent publishers to reach audiences. Link in bio.

(This one took 6 months.)
css grid named areas are the most underused feature in frontend development. I said what I said.

vitestrap v0.4 just dropped:

• File-based routing with layouts (finally)
• CSS Modules + Tailwind in the same component without them hating each other  
• Built-in RSC support (experimental)
• 30% smaller output vs v0.3

Breaking changes: zero. I'm proud of this one.

me at 22: "I'm going to learn every JavaScript framework"
me at 28: "I will learn the platform and reach for libraries only when the platform genuinely can't solve it"

the journey is part of it

People keep asking why I moved from Stanford to CERN for my visiting fellowship.

Honest answer: the problems here are just bigger. Particle physics and machine learning share the same fundamental challenge — finding signal in unimaginably large, noisy datasets. It turns out the tools translate.

New record: our Higgs event classifier hit 94.7% precision this week. For context, human physicists reviewing the same events score around 92%. 

The model isn't replacing anyone. It's giving them time to think about harder problems.
Machine learning is statistics with better PR.

March update: Posthive hit 4,200 paying customers. From 0 to default alive in 14 months. No funding, no team, just me and one contractor 3 days a week.

The product is ugly. The UX is fine. The value proposition is undeniable. That's the formula for early SaaS. #BuildInPublic

Formbird shipping update:

• Conditional logic that actually works (you're welcome)
• Payment integrations: Stripe, Razorpay, LemonSqueezy
• File uploads up to 500MB
• Custom domain support

Full launch in 2 weeks. Beta waitlist link below 👇

The scariest moment in bootstrapping isn't starting. It's the month when you've proven the idea works but you don't know if the business works. Revenue growth is flat, churn is manageable, you're not dying — but you're not growing.

You have to out-execute that month.

Product managers: stop calling everything a 'roadmap'. A list of features with rough quarters attached is not a roadmap. A roadmap has a why, a hypothesis, expected outcomes, and leading indicators. The feature list is a symptom, not a strategy.

3 questions I ask before adding anything to the roadmap:

1. What problem does this solve, and how do we know it's real?
2. Who asked for this, and are they representative of the users who generate value?
3. What does success look like, and can we measure it?

Most feature requests fail Q1.

If you're not using `satisfies` in TypeScript 5.x you are leaving type safety on the table. It combines the best parts of `as const` and type annotations and was genuinely one of the better additions in recent memory.

Chapter 3 of 'Type Everything' is now free on my site. It covers discriminated unions, narrowing, and why `any` is a code smell, not a convenience.

If it helps one developer stop using `as unknown as` I'll consider it a success.
Vercel and Netlify are having the same conversation that Heroku and App Engine were having 12 years ago. The details are different. The story is the same.

Made a generative clock that uses CSS Houdini paint worklets. Every second is a different gradient based on the current time → the color at noon is different from midnight, different from 3am.

It's completely useless and I love it.
Design tip: if you can't afford whitespace, you can't afford to ship. Spacing is not a luxury. It's the skeleton your layout hangs on.
The bar for 'good developer experience' keeps rising and I think that's excellent. Developers should have opinions about their tools. We should expect our tools to respect our time.

Just got back from ParaNetConf. The energy in the room when someone shows something actually working in production is completely different from the energy around slides and benchmarks.

Ship things. They talk louder.

Reminder that 78% of successful supply chain attacks in the last 2 years started with a single compromised npm package that had been downloaded >1M times.

Audit your dependencies. Not once — continuously.

New Cloudflare security advisory up: gradient-of-trust attacks in zero-trust architectures. Tldr — your internal services trusting other internal services implicitly is a lateral movement waiting to happen.

Link in bio. Patch notes included.

The best documentation I've ever written was for a feature no one used.

Because no one using it meant the docs *were* the product, and I had to make them genuinely teach the problem space, not just the API surface.

Gave the 'intro to distributed systems' talk again today in Madrid. 1,200 people in the room.

I still get nervous before every talk. I've given this exact talk 40 times. The nerves mean you care. The moment you stop caring is the moment you should stop speaking.

My content theory: most developer documentation reads like it was written by someone who already knew the answer.

Good documentation reads like it was written by someone who *just learned* the answer and hasn't forgotten what confused them.

The single most predictive signal of whether a B2B seed investment will work: the founder's relationship with their first 5 customers.

Not the product. Not the market. The relationship. Do they know them by name? Do those customers call them? Does the founder show up uninvited when something breaks?

Meridian's Series B closing confirms what I've seen for 2 years: infrastructure tooling for AI workflows is the most underserved category in enterprise software. James and his team built the right thing at the right time.

Three months generating art with nothing but GLSL shaders. What started as a technical exercise turned into something that I can't fully explain.

The code runs in real time. The art is different every frame. It is never the same twice. New piece tonight at midnight CET.

A generative artwork is not the output. It's the system that produced the output.

I've been trying to articulate this for 3 years and that's the shortest version I've managed.

Leadership observation: the ratio of time your team spends talking about what to build vs how to build it is a very accurate proxy for organizational health.

When the 'how' dominates, something is wrong upstream.

The infrastructure decision I'm most proud of in 4 years at Vercel wasn't the architecture. It was insisting that every engineer rotate through on-call before shipping anything to production.

You write different code when you know you're paged at 3am if it breaks.

The narrative that African developers are just 'catching up' to Western tech is nonsense. M-Pesa launched 4 years before Venmo. USSD-based services solved financial inclusion problems that Silicon Valley is still trying to solve. Pay attention.

Running the ParaNet Ghana DevConnect next month. 800 developers. 0 corporate sponsors. 100% community-funded.

That's the model. Communities that own their own spaces don't owe anyone a platform.

Shipped a big update to Dungeon.io today: procedural dialogue system. NPCs now remember your past interactions across sessions. Conversations actually evolve.

It took 4 months. It's the feature I'm most proud of in 3 years of this project.

Rust ownership model: took me 3 months to understand it in theory. Took me 1 week of writing actual Postgres extensions to internalize it.

There's a class of bugs in C and C++ that simply cannot exist in Rust. Not 'harder to write'. Cannot exist. That matters.

Stakeflow v2 audit is complete. Zero critical, one medium severity (now patched), two informational.

Audit was done by 3 independent firms simultaneously. This is the standard. Not optional.

Posthive PLG breakdown (numbers are public, Marcus approved the share):

• Free tier → paid conversion: 8.2% (industry avg: 3-5%)
• Time-to-convert: median 23 days
• Top activation trigger: first collaborative workspace invite
• #1 churn reason: didn't invite collaborators in first 7 days

The insight writes itself.

The best API documentation I've ever seen: the Stripe docs.

Not because of the content. Because of the philosophy: every page assumes you're in a hurry. The most important thing is at the top. The details are there when you need them.

That's a culture decision, not a writing decision.

Spent the last hour reading through the new Echo threads feature and thinking about the UX of deeply nested content.

There's a fascinating tension between 'more depth = more nuance' and 'more depth = harder to follow'.

The scroll position problem alone could be a master's thesis.

Shipped my first OSS project: echo-hooks — a set of React hooks for building real-time UI without fighting state management.

useLiveQuery, useOptimisticMutation, useDerivedFeed. Small scope, clean API, no dependencies on anything except React.

GitHub link 👇
Hot take I'll stand behind: component libraries that ship with Tailwind hardcoded in are anti-patterns dressed up as convenience.

Working from a new coffee shop this week. The WiFi is fine. The music is too loud. The natural light is perfect.

The 3 pillars of good remote work infrastructure are: connection, quiet, and light. Most places get 2 out of 3.

Replying to @jordanblake — the scroll position problem in nested threads is actually a hard UX research question.

We published a small internal study at ParaNetLabs last year: users' mental model of 'where am I in this conversation' breaks down after about 3 levels. The visual metaphor needs to change, not just the depth limit.
Replying to @jordanblake — have you looked at how Linear handles this? The inline subtask expansion pattern is the closest thing I've seen to a solution that doesn't sacrifice readability.

Building in public month 14:

Revenue: $24,800 MRR
New customers: 312
Churn: 4.1%
Biggest win: first enterprise contract ($4,800/yr)
Biggest loss: co-founder conversation that didn't work out

The enterprise contract changes everything about how I think about the product. #BuildInPublic

Typography in interfaces rule #1: your body text should be legible at 3am by someone who is half asleep and slightly stressed.

If it isn't, nothing else about the design matters.

Current project: a CSS grid art generator where the layout algorithm is a modified Mondrian process.

The results shouldn't be interesting. They are. This is why I got into generative design.
Controversial: the CSS Cascade is not a bug. It's a feature that most developers never learned because they were too busy installing PostCSS plugins.

Snaplane beta feedback summary after 2 weeks:

Top requested: markdown support ✅ (added)
Top requested: keyboard shortcuts ✅ (added)
Top complaint: onboarding took 8 minutes ✅ (now 2 minutes)
Top unexplained churn: people who never customized settings

The product is talking. Listening.

There are 3,400 journalists worldwide whose primary beat is technology. Less than 200 of them have ever written code professionally. Less than 80 of them understand how a neural network actually works at a mathematical level.

This is not an accusation. It's a resource allocation problem.

Year 1 as a founder: product-market fit feels like a goal.
Year 2: product-market fit feels like a hypothesis.
Year 3: product-market fit feels like a question you never fully answer, just continuously re-earn.

Year 5: you stop talking about it and start living it or not.

Something that keeps me up at night: the datasets most ML models are trained on reflect the documented world. Most human knowledge is undocumented. We're building models that know more about Wikipedia than about how things actually work in practice.

The single most valuable engineering practice I've implemented across three companies:

Post-mortems that require a 'what did the system learn' section in addition to the 'what did humans do wrong' section.

Systems have failure modes. Those failure modes are information.

If you're an eng who doesn't write docs: the code you're most proud of is invisible to 90% of the people who could benefit from it.

Documentation is distribution.

New piece: 'The Case for Local-First Community Infrastructure' — why every developer community should control its own platform rather than depending on a tech company's goodwill.

Link in bio. Longread warning: 6,000 words.

The technical art piece I'm most proud of: a shader that takes a photo as input and outputs a new image where every pixel's color is computed by a fragment shader that queries the surrounding pixels recursively.

It's technically 'just an image filter'. It's not.

Your authentication system is probably fine. Your authorization system is probably not.

Most security breaches I've seen weren't about bad passwords. They were about an authenticated user doing something they shouldn't have been allowed to do.

PostgreSQL 18 beta has been running in our staging environment for 3 weeks. The async I/O changes are real. Query throughput up 40% on our heaviest workloads with zero application code changes.

Staging ≠ prod but this is promising.

Writing advice that applies equally to documentation, technical blog posts, and API error messages:

Write the sentence. Then cut the first five words. You probably started with a throat-clear.

B2B SaaS benchmark thread for early-stage companies:

• DAU/MAU > 25% = healthy engagement
• NPS > 50 at $1k-10k ACV = strong product
• Payback period < 18 months = viable economics  
• Logo churn < 10% = retention is working
• Quick ratio > 4 = efficient growth engine

Save this. Revisit quarterly.

PSA: if your developer documentation has a 'Getting Started' guide that takes more than 10 minutes to complete, that's not a 'Getting Started' guide.

That's a hiring challenge.

The PM anti-pattern I see most often in B2B startups: treating enterprise customers as the whole market.

Your enterprise customer has a procurement process, a legal team, and a 12-month sales cycle. They are not your product's natural habitat at $1M ARR.

The DeFi composability problem isn't technical — every major protocol is well-engineered. The problem is that composability creates dependency chains that make it impossible to reason about systemic risk.

This is a coordination problem disguised as an engineering problem.

Game dev lesson I keep relearning: the feature that's hardest to cut is usually the feature that's hurting the game the most.

We hold on to our clever ideas. Players don't care about clever. They care about fun.

Q1 portfolio check-in. 14 of 17 active portfolio companies grew revenue QoQ. The three that didn't are in the process of pivoting, which is what they should be doing.

The market for AI infrastructure is bifurcating fast: companies that can deploy and companies that can sell. Different skills.

What @mayachen said about mental models breaking down at 3 levels of nesting is exactly it — the spatial reasoning required shifts from reading to navigation.

Wonder if there's a design pattern that acknowledges this transition explicitly rather than fighting it.

The documentation I'm most proud of at Stripe isn't the technical reference. It's the conceptual overview we wrote for engineers who are new to payments.

Two years later, that doc gets cited in support tickets more often than the API reference. The explanation outlived the API version.

The best thing you can do for your company culture in the first 18 months is hire three people who would work together without you in the room.

Not 'great individually'. Three people who make each other better in specific ways.

The reason I got into open source: I had a bug that was clearly in the library, not my code. Opened an issue. Author responded in 8 minutes with a patch.

The idea that strangers would spend their Saturday helping me fix my stupid problem changed how I thought about what software could be.

Design opinion: most product interfaces would be better with half the font sizes and twice the whitespace.

Clarity is not achieved by making things bigger. It's achieved by making fewer things compete.

New design principle I'm testing: 'The Embarrassment Test'.

Before shipping any UI, ask: would I be embarrassed to show this to a senior designer I respect?

Not because you need their approval. Because the answer tells you if you actually know what you shipped.

After 1 year of weekly build-in-public updates:

The people who DM me asking for the secret are usually looking for a shortcut.

The people who read every update and then stop trying to find a shortcut — those are the ones who end up shipping.

Context: the paper that kicked off the modern transformer era was published in 2017. It's 2026. We are 9 years into what will probably be a 50-year infrastructure build.

The 'we need to solve alignment now' crowd and the 'this is just fancy autocomplete' crowd are both wrong in ways that will seem obvious in 10 years.

Correction to my piece from Tuesday: the funding round was $28M, not $280M. I was off by exactly one decimal place.

I've issued a correction and I want to be transparent: this happened because I trusted a secondary source without verification. Won't happen again.

The transition from 'writing code that solves your problem' to 'writing code that is easy for others to change' is not a skill upgrade. It's almost a different skill entirely.

Most developers learn the first and think they know the second.

Still getting DMs about the View Transitions API explainer I wrote 18 months ago. The web platform has never moved this fast. The tools are catching up to what designers have been asking for since 2012.

Friendly reminder: your threat model is not about what's been exploited in the past. It's about what your adversary is motivated to exploit next.

The attack surface changes. Your model should too.

People ask why I moved back to Dublin from London.

Honest answer: the tech scene here is 10 years behind London's in scale, but the community is 10 years ahead in quality. The signal-to-noise ratio is better. I think clearer.

The best community-building advice I've been given: your community is not your audience. The moment you treat community members as recipients of your content rather than co-authors of the shared experience, you've stopped building a community.

Three things I wish someone told me before I started giving talks:

1. Audience members are hoping you succeed
2. Slow down by 30% from what feels natural
3. The questions after the talk matter more than the talk

The talk is the invitation. The conversation is the point.
